---
title: "Logical reasoning"
date: 2025-02-17
author: "Edoardo \"Dado\" Marcora"
format:
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
    incremental: true
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
    execute:
      echo: true
      eval: true
      warning: false
      fragment: true
    output-location: fragment
df-print: kable
---

## Inference

-   Inference is logical reasoning

## Scientific reasoning is inductive reasoning

XXX

## Statistical inference

-   Statistical inference is inductive inference using incomplete data and a model of the data generating process (DGP) as evidence

XXX

[![Dosso Dossi: Allegory of Fortune (left, the god of chance; right, the goddess of luck)](images/dosso_dossi.allegory_of_fortune.png)](https://www.getty.edu/art/collection/object/103RJG)

![Raphael: The School of Athens (left, Plato; right, Aristotle)](images/Sanzio_01_Plato_Aristotle.jpg){fig-alt="https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=75881"}

## Statistical inference

![](images/Slide1.png)

[Shmueli (2010)](https://doi.org/10.1214/10-STS330) To Explain or to Predict?

## Example data (and frequentist inference)

[**LPS treatment blunts UCP1 expression in subcutaneous adipocytes**]{.orange}

![](images/41467_2020_15483_Fig1_HTML.png)

Values are expressed as mean ± SEM. \*\**p* = 0.004. Statistical test used: two-sided *t*-test.

 

**Figure 1** from [ASK1 inhibits browning of white adipose tissue in obesity](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-15483-7)

Lucchini *et al.* (2020) **Nat Commun** 11(1):1642

## Statistical inference

![](images/clipboard-2620420083.png)

## Causal inference

![](images/clipboard-1233462405.png)

## Knowledge-based AI

-   Logic (e.g., propositional logic or first-order logic) is a language used to represent knowledge and to reason (draw conclusions, make inferences) based on this knowledge

-   Logic is used in a knowledge-based AI agent to represent knowledge and to reason (draw conclusions, make inferences) based on this knowledge

-   In deductive logic the goal is to determine whether an argument is valid (the premises entail the conclusion), using some inference algorithm or rules

-   In inductive logic the goal is to determine whether an argument is strong (the premises partially entail the conclusion), using some inference algorithm or rules

## Deductive inference: Propositional logic

-   The goal is to **prove** that a conclusion/hypothesis/query is true given some premises/evidence/knowledge known or assumed to be true

-   $E \models C$

-   $P(C \mid E) = 1$

## Arguments/Rules of deductive inference

Knowledge-based AI capable of logical reasoning

Knowledge engineering

Argument forms as inference rules of propositional logic/deductive inference:

-   *Modus ponens*

-   And elimination rule

-   Unit resolution rule

-   Double negation elimination rule

-   Implication elimination rule

-   Biconditional elimination rule

-   De Morgan's law (1)

-   De Morgan's law (2)

-   Distributive law (1)

-   Distributive law (2)

-   *Modus tollens*

## Propositional logic

XXX

## Propositional symbols/variables

In propositional logic, the terms **propositional symbol** and **propositional variable** are often used interchangeably to denote **atomic propositions**—statements that are either true or false and cannot be broken down into simpler statements. These atomic propositions serve as the foundational building blocks for more complex, compound propositions formed using logical connectives/boolean operators such as AND, OR, and NOT.

## Setup environment

```{r}
#| output-location: default
#| output: false
library(tidyverse)
library(magrittr)
library(readxl)
library(mosaic)
library(ggformula)
library(latex2exp)
library(flexplot)
library(broom)
library(easystats)
library(extraDistr)

theme_set(theme_minimal())

# for bayesian inference
library(rstanarm)
library(posterior)
library(bayesplot)
```

## Observations

```{r}
#| include: false
d.obs <- read_xlsx("41467_2020_15483_MOESM4_ESM.xlsx", sheet = 1, range = "A25:D26", col_names = c("Treatment", "R1", "R2", "R3"))
```

```{r}
#| include: false
d.obs %<>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Treatment, names_to = "Replicate", values_to = "UCP1") %>% 
  mutate(Treatment = fct_relevel(Treatment, "Saline"), UCP1 = UCP1) %>%
  select(-Replicate)
```

```{r}
#| include: false
d.obs %<>% mutate(X = as.numeric(Treatment) - 1, Y = round(UCP1 * 10, 0)) %>% select(X, Y)
```

```{r}
#| include: false
ss <-
  expand_grid(
    X = c(0, 1),
    Y = seq(0, 15)) %>% 
  mutate(Ω = paste0("⍵", 1:n())) %>%
  select(Ω, everything())

ss
```

```{r}
#| output-location: default
d.obs
```

## Process model

::: nonincremental
-   Given a process model (a.k.a. *data model*, *generative model*, or *likelihood*):
:::

$$p(x, y \mid M)$$

## Process model

::: nonincremental
-   Given a process model (a.k.a. *data model*, *generative model*, or *likelihood*):
:::

$$p(x, y \mid \theta, M^*)$$

## Process model

::: nonincremental
-   Given a process model (a.k.a. *data model*, *generative model*, or *likelihood*):
:::

$$p(x, y \mid \theta)$$

-   Assuming $p(x \mid \theta) = p(x)$:

::: fragment
$$p(x, y \mid \theta) \propto p(y \mid x, \theta)$$
:::

-   Assuming the standard linear regression model $M_{L+E_N}$:

::: fragment
$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x + \epsilon \quad \epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma)$$
:::

-   The regression likelihood $p(y \mid x, \theta)$ is:

::: fragment
$$p(y \mid x, \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma, M_{L+E_N})$$
:::

## Process model

::: nonincremental
-   Given a process model (a.k.a. *data model*, *generative model*, or *likelihood*):

$$p(x, y \mid \theta)$$

-   Assuming $p(x \mid \theta) = p(x)$:

$$p(x, y \mid \theta) \propto p(y \mid x, \theta)$$

-   Assuming the standard linear regression model $M_{L+E_N}$:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x + \epsilon \quad \epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma)$$

-   The regression likelihood $p(y \mid x, \theta)$ is:
:::

$$p(y \mid \mu, \sigma, M_N) \quad \mu = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x$$

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   The regression likelihood $p(y \mid x, \theta)$ is:
:::

$$p(y \mid \mu, \sigma, M_N) \quad \mu = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x$$

::: fragment
$$Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma) \quad \mu = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x$$
:::

::: fragment
$$p(y \mid \mu, \sigma, M_N) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{y - \mu}{\sigma} \right)^2}$$
:::

::: fragment
$$p(y \mid x, \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma, M_{L+E_N}) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{y - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x)}{\sigma} \right)^2}$$
:::

::: {.text-align-center .fragment}
`p = dnorm(Y, mean = b0 + b1 * X, sd = sigma)`
:::

## Standard linear regression model 2: Visualization

$$p(y \mid x, \beta_0 = 10, \beta_1 = -7, \sigma = 1)$$

```{r}
#| output-location: column
ss %>%
  mutate(
    b0 = 10,
    b1 = -7,
    sigma = 1,
    mu = b0 + b1 * X) %>%
  mutate(p = ddnorm(Y, mean = mu, sd = sigma)) %>%

  

      
  gf_tile(Y ~ X, fill = ~ p, color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) %>% gf_labs(title = latex2exp::TeX(r"($p(y \ |\ x, M_{2^*})$)")) %>%
  gf_text(label = ~ sprintf("%0.4f", p)) %>% gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue", limits = c(0, 1)), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 1)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)))
```

## Standard linear regression model 2: Sampling

$$p(y \mid x, \beta_0 = 10, \beta_1 = -7, \sigma = 1)$$

```{r}
#| echo: false
set.seed(666)
```

```{r}
#| output-location: column
ss %>%
  mutate(
    b0 = 10,
    b1 = -7,
    sigma = 1,
    mu = b0 + b1 * X) %>%
  mutate(p = ddnorm(Y, mean = mu, sd = sigma)) %>%
  slice_sample(n = 3, by = X, weight_by = p, replace = TRUE) %>%

  
  
  
  gf_jitter(Y ~ factor(X), width = 0.25, height = 0) %>% gf_labs(title = latex2exp::TeX(r"($n = 6$ data points $\left(x_i, y_i\right)$ sampled with replacement from $p(x, y \ |\ M_{2^*})$)"), x = "X") %>% gf_refine(scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 15), breaks = seq(0, 15), minor_breaks = NULL))
```

## Standard linear regression model 2: Sims vs obs

$$p(y \mid x, \beta_0 = 10, \beta_1 = -7, \sigma = 1)$$

```{r}
#| echo: false
set.seed(666)
```

```{r}
#| output-location: column
ss %>%
  mutate(
    b0 = 10,
    b1 = -7,
    sigma = 1,
    mu = b0 + b1 * X) %>%
  mutate(p = ddnorm(Y, mean = mu, sd = sigma)) %>%
  slice_sample(n = 3, by = X, weight_by = p, replace = TRUE) %>%

  
  
  
  gf_jitter(Y ~ factor(X), width = 0.25, height = 0) %>% gf_jitter(Y ~ factor(X), data = d.obs, width = 0.25, height = 0, color = "red") %>% gf_labs(title = latex2exp::TeX(r"($n = 6$ data points $\left(x_i, y_i\right)$ sampled with replacement from $p(y \ |\ x, M_{2^*})$ + observations in red)"), x = "X") %>% gf_refine(scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 15), breaks = seq(0, 15), minor_breaks = NULL))
```

## Process model and statistical inference

::: nonincremental
-   Given a process model (a.k.a. *data model*, *generative model*, or *likelihood*):
:::

$$p(x, y \mid \theta)$$

-   The corresponding inference model is:

::: fragment
$$p(\theta \mid x, y) = \frac{p(\theta) \cdot p(x, y \mid \theta)}{p(x, y)}$$
:::

-   $p(\theta \mid x, y)$ is called the **posterior distribution**

-   $p(\theta)$ is called the **prior distribution**

-   $p(x, y \mid \theta)$ is called the **likelihood**

-   $p(x, y)$ is a normalizing constant a.k.a. the **marginal likelihood** $= \sum_\theta p(x, y \mid \theta)$

::: fragment
$$p(\theta \mid x, y) \propto p(\theta) \cdot p(x, y \mid \theta)$$
:::

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

::: nonincremental
-   The inference model:
:::

$$p(\theta \mid x, y) \propto p(\theta) \cdot p(x, y \mid \theta)$$

-   Assuming a standard linear regression model:

::: fragment
$$p(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma \mid x, y) \propto p(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma) \cdot p(y \mid x, \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma)$$
:::

-   Assuming $p(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma) = p(\beta_0) \cdot p(\beta_1) \cdot p(\sigma)$:

::: fragment
$$p(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma \mid x, y) \propto p(\beta_0) \cdot p(\beta_1) \cdot p(\sigma) \cdot p(y \mid x, \beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma)$$
:::

::: fragment
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| output-location: default
prior = d?(b0) * d?(b1) * d?(sigma)

likelihood = dnorm(Y, mean = b0 + b1 * X, sd = sigma)

posterior.unnormalized = prior * likelihood

posterior = posterior.unnormalized / sum(posterior.unnormalized)
```
:::

## Prior probability distribution

::: nonincremental
-   Assuming the prior probability distribution of parameter $\beta_0$ is:
:::

::: fragment
$$
p(\beta_0, \text{min} = 0, \text{max} = 15, M_U) \quad \beta_0 \sim U(\text{min} = 0, \text{max} = 15)
$$
:::

::: fragment
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| output-location: default
p = dunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15)
```
:::

-   Assuming the prior probability distribution of parameter $\beta_1$ is:

::: fragment
$$
p(\beta_1, \text{min} = -15, \text{max} = 15, M_U) \quad \beta_1 \sim U(\text{min} = -15, \text{max} = 15)
$$
:::

::: fragment
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| output-location: default
p = dunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15)
```
:::

-   Assuming the prior probability distribution of parameter $\sigma$ is:

::: fragment
$$
p(\sigma, \text{min} = 1, \text{max} = 6, M_U) \quad \sigma \sim U(\text{min} = 1, \text{max} = 6)
$$
:::

::: fragment
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| output-location: default
p = dunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6)
```
:::

## Prior probability distribution

::: nonincremental
-   The joint prior probability distribution of parameters $\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, and $\sigma$ is:
:::

$$p(\beta_0, \text{min} = 0, \text{max} = 15, M_U) \cdot p(\beta_1, \text{min} = -15, \text{max} = 15, M_U) \cdot p(\sigma, \text{min} = 1, \text{max} = 6, M_U)$$

::: fragment
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| output-location: default
prior = dunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * dunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15) * dunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6)
```
:::

## Sample space

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss <-
  expand_grid(
    b0 = seq(0, 15),
    b1 = seq(-15, 15),
    sigma = seq(1, 6)) %>% 
  mutate(Ω = paste0("⍵", 1:n())) %>%
  select(Ω, everything())

nrow(ss)

head(ss)
```

## Prior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    prior = ddunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * ddunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15) * ddunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  head()
```

## Prior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    prior = ddunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * ddunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15) * ddunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ prior, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue", limits = c(0, 1)), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4)))
```

## Prior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  mutate(prior = 1 / n()) %>%
  head()
```

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   Compute likelihood of a single data point for each combination of parameters:
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  mutate(
    X = 0,
    Y = 11) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    mu = b0 + b1 * X,
    likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  head()
```

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   Compute likelihood of a single data point for each combination of parameters:
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %>%
  mutate(
    X = 0,
    Y = 11) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    mu = b0 + b1 * X,
    likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ likelihood, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   Compute likelihood of a single data point for each combination of parameters:
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %>%
  mutate(
    X = 1,
    Y = 3) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    mu = b0 + b1 * X,
    likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ likelihood, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   Compute likelihood of multiple data points for each combination of parameter, assuming observations are **independent and identically distributed** (i.i.d.):
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %>%
  mutate(
    data = list(d.obs) # add a list column containing d.obs in each row
  ) %>% 
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    likelihood = {
      data %>%
        mutate(
          mu = b0 + b1 * X,
          likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)
        ) %>%
        pull(likelihood) %>%
        prod()
    }
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  head()
```

## Standard linear regression likelihood

::: nonincremental
-   Compute likelihood of multiple data points for each combination of parameter, assuming observations are **independent and identically distributed** (i.i.d.):
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %>%
  mutate(
    data = list(d.obs) # add a list column containing d.obs in each row
  ) %>% 
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    likelihood = {
      data %>%
        mutate(
          mu = b0 + b1 * X,
          likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)
        ) %>%
        pull(likelihood) %>%
        prod()
    }
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ likelihood, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Posterior probability distribution

::: nonincremental
-   Compute posterior probability distribution by multiplying prior and likelihood:
:::

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %<>%
  mutate(
    data = list(d.obs) # add a list column containing d.obs in each row
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    prior = ddunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * ddunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15) * ddunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6),
    likelihood = {
      data %>%
        mutate(
          mu = b0 + b1 * X,
          likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)
        ) %>%
        pull(likelihood) %>%
        prod()
    },
    posterior.unnormalized = prior * likelihood
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(posterior = posterior.unnormalized / sum(posterior.unnormalized))

ss %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ posterior, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Marginal prior probability distributions

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(prior = sum(prior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(prior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Marginal posterior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(posterior = sum(posterior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(posterior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Plot prior lines

```{r}
#| output-location: default
# sample 1000 parameter combinations/lines from the posterior
sampled_params <- ss %>% slice_sample(n = 1000, weight_by = prior, replace = TRUE)
```

 

```{r}
#| echo: false
set.seed(666)
```

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
# generate two data points for each sampled line
line_data <- sampled_params %>%
  crossing(X = c(0, 1)) %>%
  mutate(Y = b0 + b1 * X)

# plot observed data and sampled lines
gf_line(Y ~ X, group = ~ interaction(b0, b1), data = line_data, 
          color = "gray", alpha = 0.5) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-0.5, 1.5), y = c(0, 15)) %>%
  gf_refine(
    theme(
      panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major gridlines
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor gridlines
      panel.background = element_blank(),  # Optional: Remove panel background
      axis.line = element_line(color = "gray")  # Add axis lines for clarity
    )
  )
```

## Plot posterior lines

```{r}
#| output-location: default
# sample 1000 parameter combinations/lines from the posterior
sampled_params <- ss %>% slice_sample(n = 1000, weight_by = posterior, replace = TRUE)
```

 

```{r}
#| echo: false
set.seed(666)
```

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
# generate two data points for each sampled line
line_data <- sampled_params %>%
  crossing(X = c(0, 1)) %>%
  mutate(Y = b0 + b1 * X)

# plot observed data and sampled lines
gf_jitter(Y ~ X, data = d.obs, color = "red", size = 2, width = 0.1) %>%
  gf_line(Y ~ X, group = ~ interaction(b0, b1), data = line_data, 
          color = "gray", alpha = 0.5) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-0.5, 1.5), y = c(0, 15)) %>%
  gf_refine(
    theme(
      panel.grid.major = element_blank(),  # Remove major gridlines
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # Remove minor gridlines
      panel.background = element_blank(),  # Optional: Remove panel background
      axis.line = element_line(color = "gray")  # Add axis lines for clarity
    )
  )
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
posterior_draws <- ss %>%
  slice_sample(n = 1000, weight_by = posterior, replace = TRUE) %>%
  select(b0, b1, sigma) %>% 
  as_draws_df()

summarize_draws(posterior_draws)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
describe_posterior(posterior_draws)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_hist(posterior_draws, binwidth = 1)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_intervals(posterior_draws)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output: false
stan_glm.fit <- stan_glm(Y ~ X, data = d.obs, family = gaussian())
```

 

```{r}
#| output-location: default
summary(stan_glm.fit)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
summarize_draws(stan_glm.fit)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
describe_posterior(stan_glm.fit)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_hist(stan_glm.fit, binwidth = 1)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_intervals(stan_glm.fit)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
rope(stan_glm.fit, parameters = "X") %>% plot()
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
rope(stan_glm.fit, parameters = "X")
```

## Posterior with uniform priors

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %<>%
  mutate(
    data = list(d.obs) # add a list column containing d.obs in each row
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    prior = ddunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * ddunif(b1, min = -15, max = 15) * ddunif(sigma, min = 1, max = 6),
    likelihood = {
      data %>%
        mutate(
          mu = b0 + b1 * X,
          likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)
        ) %>%
        pull(likelihood) %>%
        prod()
    },
    posterior.unnormalized = prior * likelihood
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(posterior = posterior.unnormalized / sum(posterior.unnormalized))

ss %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ posterior, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Marginal prior probability distributions

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(prior = sum(prior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(prior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Marginal posterior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(posterior = sum(posterior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(posterior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Posterior with non-uniform priors

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
ss %<>%
  mutate(
    data = list(d.obs) # add a list column containing d.obs in each row
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    prior = ddunif(b0, min = 0, max = 15) * ddnorm(b1, mean = 0, sd = 4) * ddgamma(sigma, shape = 1, scale = 2),
    likelihood = {
      data %>%
        mutate(
          mu = b0 + b1 * X,
          likelihood = ddnorm(Y, mu, sigma)
        ) %>%
        pull(likelihood) %>%
        prod()
    },
    posterior.unnormalized = prior * likelihood
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(posterior = posterior.unnormalized / sum(posterior.unnormalized))

ss %>%
  gf_tile(b0 ~ b1 | sigma, fill = ~ posterior, color = "black", linewidth = 0.1) %>%
  gf_refine(scale_fill_continuous(low = "white", high = "blue"), scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-15, 15)), scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15)), theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.text = element_text(size = 4))) # auto scale p for better viz
```

## Marginal prior probability distributions

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(prior = sum(prior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(prior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Marginal posterior probability distribution

```{r}
#| output-location: default
ss %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(b0, b1, sigma), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(parameter, value) %>%
  summarise(posterior = sum(posterior), .groups = "drop") %>%
  gf_line(posterior ~ value | parameter, color = ~ parameter) %>%
  gf_facet_grid(parameter ~ .) %>%
  gf_lims(x = c(-15, 15), y = c(0, 0.5)) %>%
  gf_refine(theme(legend.position = "none"))
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
posterior_draws <- ss %>%
  slice_sample(n = 1000, weight_by = posterior, replace = TRUE) %>%
  select(b0, b1, sigma) %>% 
  as_draws_df()

summarize_draws(posterior_draws)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
describe_posterior(posterior_draws)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_hist(posterior_draws, binwidth = 1)
```

## Bayesian inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
mcmc_intervals(posterior_draws)
```

## Frequentist inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: default
lm.fit <- lm(Y ~ X, data = d.obs)

summary(lm.fit)
```

##  {background-image="images/thats_all_folks.jpg" background-size="50%"}

## Frequentist inference using the linear model

```{r}
#| output-location: slide
params_df <- broom::tidy(lm.fit, conf.int = TRUE)
sigma <- summary(lm.fit)$sigma
n <- nrow(d.obs)
df <- get_df(lm.fit)

conf.low <- sigma * sqrt(df / qchisq(0.975, df))
conf.high <- sigma * sqrt(df / qchisq(0.025, df))

sigma_row <- tibble(
  term = "sigma",
  estimate = sigma,
  conf.low = conf.low,
  conf.high = conf.high
)

params_df <- bind_rows(params_df, sigma_row)

gf_errorbar(conf.low + conf.high ~ term, data = params_df, width = 0.25) %>%
  gf_point(estimate ~ term) %>%
  gf_refine(coord_flip())
```

## Posterior predictive distribution

```{r}
#| eval: false
posterior_predictive <- posterior_draws %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    Y_pred = list(
      d.obs %>%
        mutate(
          Y_sim = b0 + b1 * X + runif(n(), min = -s, max = s)  # predict Y with uniform noise
          # Y_sim = b0 + b1 * X + rnorm(n(), mean = 0, sd = s)  # predict Y with normal noise
        )
    )
  ) %>%
  unnest(Y_pred)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
posterior_summary <- posterior_predictive %>%
  group_by(X) %>%
  summarise(
    Y_mean = mean(Y_sim),
    Y_lower = quantile(Y_sim, 0.025),
    Y_upper = quantile(Y_sim, 0.975)
  )

posterior_summary
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot() +
  # Observed data points
  geom_jitter(data = d.obs, aes(x = X, y = Y), color = "red", width = 0.25) +
  # Posterior predictive intervals
  geom_ribbon(
    data = posterior_summary,
    aes(x = X, ymin = Y_lower, ymax = Y_upper),
    fill = "blue", alpha = 0.2
  ) +
  # Posterior predictive mean
  geom_line(data = posterior_summary, aes(x = X, y = Y_mean), color = "blue", size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Distribution",
       x = "X",
       y = "Y") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Simulate posterior predictive distribution
posterior_predictive_diff <- posterior_draws %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    # Simulated predictions for X = 0 and X = 1
    Y_pred_0 = list(b0 + b1 * 0 + runif(1000, min = -s, max = s)),  # For X = 0
    Y_pred_1 = list(b0 + b1 * 1 + runif(1000, min = -s, max = s))   # For X = 1
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Compute the mean difference between X = 1 and X = 0 for each posterior draw
    diff = mean(unlist(Y_pred_1) - unlist(Y_pred_0))
  ) %>%
  ungroup()
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Summarize the differences
summary_diff <- posterior_predictive_diff %>%
  summarise(
    point_estimate = mean(diff),           # Posterior mean of the difference
    lower_ci = quantile(diff, 0.025),      # 2.5% credible interval
    upper_ci = quantile(diff, 0.975)       # 97.5% credible interval
  )
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Plot the posterior distribution of the difference
ggplot(posterior_predictive_diff, aes(x = diff)) +
  geom_density(fill = "blue", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = summary_diff$point_estimate), color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = summary_diff$lower_ci), color = "black", linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = summary_diff$upper_ci), color = "black", linetype = "dotted") +
  labs(
    title = "Posterior Predictive Difference (X=1 - X=0) with Uniform Error",
    x = "Difference",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
